revisions.content:
'''DNA''', or deoxyribonucleic acid, is the molecule in living things that

contains the coding information for creating [[protein|proteins]].  It is

also the molecule of heredity--whenever an organism reproduces, each

offspring gets a copy of its parents' DNA.



Structurally, nucleic acids are giant [[polymer|polymers]] composed of units called

[[nucleotide|nucleotides]], each of which consists of a [[sugar]], a [[phosphate]], and a

[[base]].  The sugar in DNA is [[deoxyribose]].  The four bases are

[[adenine]], [[cytosine]], [[thymine]], and [[guanine]].  Each can readily

form [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonds]] to one of the others--A with T, C with G--so that two

matching strands of DNA will group together in a double helix.  This form is

called B-DNA; there are a few others.



Reasons why [[thymine]] is used in DNA, and [[uracil]] in [[RNA]] need some explanation.

Thymine is created from uracil in a [[methylation]] process, which is energetically expensive.

In other words, uracil is cheaper for a cell that thymine.

Unfortunatelly [[cytosine]] becomes [[deamination|deaminated]] with significant speed and becomes [[uracil]].

If uracil were used in DNA, that would be source of many mutations.

With thymine, all uracils created in such way can be removed, and the proper fragment of DNA can be copied from the other strand.

Cheaper uracil is used in RNA because RNA doesn't need to last a long time anyway.



DNA does not code for any proteins directly.  Instead, matching strands of

messenger [[RNA]] (mRNA) are formed by [[enzyme|enzymes]] called

[[RNA polymerase|RNA polymerases]]--this is called "transcription"--which

are sent off into the [[cytoplasm]] for

translation in the [[ribosome|ribosomes]].  Other kinds of RNA are also formed

to help in the process--tRNA, rRNA, and snRNA. The [[genetic code]] used by these molecules to

encode the sequence of proteins is highly conserved, nearly universally so.

This suggests that all life on [[Earth]] is descended from a single original

strain of living matter.



In bacteria there is a single main strand of DNA, copied over when the cell

replicates.  There may also be small pieces of circular DNA called

[[plasmid|plasmids]] floating in the cytoplasm that can be exchanged between cells.

In [[eukaryote|eukaryotes]] there are typically multiple pieces of DNA contained in a

central [[cell nucleus|nucleus]], separated out in cell division by a

process called [[mitosis]].  There may be a single copy of the [[genome]]

(whence the organism is [[haploid]]), two copies

([[diploid]]), or many copies ([[polyploid]]).



In preparation for [[Sexual Reproduction|sexual reproduction]], haploid

cells are produced from a diploid precursor in a process called [[meiosis]].

Eventually some haploid cells unite together to form a new diploid cell

([[syngamy]]) with half its genome from each diploid ancestor.  When there

are different versions of the same gene, their effects may be blended or a

single version may be dominant.



See also: [[Ploidy]], [[Biology]], [[Genetics]].



/Talk

'''DNA''', or deoxyribonucleic acid, is the molecule in living things that contains the coding information for creating [[protein|proteins]]. It is also the molecule of heredity--whenever an organism reproduces, each offspring gets a copy of its parents' DNA. Structurally, nucleic acids are giant [[polymer|polymers]] composed of units called [[nucleotide|nucleotides]], each of which consists of a [[sugar]], a [[phosphate]], and a [[base]]. The sugar in DNA is [[deoxyribose]]. The four bases are [[adenine]], [[cytosine]], [[thymine]], and [[guanine]]. Each can readily form [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonds]] to one of the others--A with T, C with G--so that two matching strands of DNA will group together in a double helix. This form is called B-DNA; there are a few others. Reasons why [[thymine]] is used in DNA, and [[uracil]] in [[RNA]] need some explanation. Thymine is created from uracil in a [[methylation]] process, which is energetically expensive. In other words, uracil is cheaper for a cell that thymine. Unfortunatelly [[cytosine]] becomes [[deamination|deaminated]] with significant speed and becomes [[uracil]]. If uracil were used in DNA, that would be source of many mutations. With thymine, all uracils created in such way can be removed, and the proper fragment of DNA can be copied from the other strand. Cheaper uracil is used in RNA because RNA doesn't need to last a long time anyway. DNA does not code for any proteins directly. Instead, matching strands of messenger [[RNA]] (mRNA) are formed by [[enzyme|enzymes]] called [[RNA polymerase|RNA polymerases]]--this is called "transcription"--which are sent off into the [[cytoplasm]] for translation in the [[ribosome|ribosomes]]. Other kinds of RNA are also formed to help in the process--tRNA, rRNA, and snRNA. The [[genetic code]] used by these molecules to encode the sequence of proteins is highly conserved, nearly universally so. This suggests that all life on [[Earth]] is descended from a single original strain of living matter. In bacteria there is a single main strand of DNA, copied over when the cell replicates. There may also be small pieces of circular DNA called [[plasmid|plasmids]] floating in the cytoplasm that can be exchanged between cells. In [[eukaryote|eukaryotes]] there are typically multiple pieces of DNA contained in a central [[cell nucleus|nucleus]], separated out in cell division by a process called [[mitosis]]. There may be a single copy of the [[genome]] (whence the organism is [[haploid]]), two copies ([[diploid]]), or many copies ([[polyploid]]). In preparation for [[Sexual Reproduction|sexual reproduction]], haploid cells are produced from a diploid precursor in a process called [[meiosis]]. Eventually some haploid cells unite together to form a new diploid cell ([[syngamy]]) with half its genome from each diploid ancestor. When there are different versions of the same gene, their effects may be blended or a single version may be dominant. See also: [[Ploidy]], [[Biology]], [[Genetics]]. The discovery of DNA
The structure of nucleic acids was discovered in the C19. Later it was found that the sugar in nucleic acid could be either ribose ([[RNA]]) or deoxyribose (DNA). In 1943, [[Oswald Avery]] proved that DNA carries genetic information and suggested DNA might actually be the gene. In 1948, [[Linus Pauling]] discovered that many proteins take the shape of an alpha helix. In 1950, biochemist [[Erwin Chargaff]] found that the arrangement of bases in DNA varied, but the amount of certain bases always occurred in a one-to-one ratio. In England two separate investigations into DNA took place in the 1950s. At [[Cambridge University]], [[Francis Crick]] and [[James Watson]] were making physical models to eventually create an accurate picture of the molecule. At [[Kings College]] in [[London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining X-ray diffraction images of DNA. In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date. She had found that DNA can exist in two forms. From this she had deduced that the phosphate part of the molecule was on the outside. Based on an imperfect recollection of her information, Watson and Crick made a failed model. Franklin had also found that the X-ray diffractions of one of the forms of DNA had the characteristics of a helix. She suspected that DNA was helical but did not announce this. Watson and Crick made a crucial step, suggesting the molecule was made of two nucleotide chains, each in a helix as Franklin had found, but one going up and the other going down. Crick learned of the work of Chargaff in 1952. He added that to the model, so that matching base pairs interlocked to keep the distance between the chains constant. This model showed that each strand of the DNA molecule was a template for the other. The fit with experimental data was such that it was almost immediately accepted. Their paper 'A structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid' was published in April [[1953]]. further research uncovered how DNa made proteins in 1957. In [[1962]], Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the [[Nobel Prize]] for physiology/medicine, Franklin had died. ---- [[talk:DNA|/Talk]]

'''DNA''', or deoxyribonucleic acid, is the molecule in living things that contains the coding information for creating [[protein|proteins]]. It is also the molecule of heredity--whenever an organism reproduces, each offspring gets a copy of its parents' DNA. Structurally, nucleic acids are giant [[polymer|polymers]] composed of units called [[nucleotide|nucleotides]], each of which consists of a [[sugar]], a [[phosphate]], and a [[base]]. The sugar in DNA is [[deoxyribose]]. The four bases are [[adenine]], [[cytosine]], [[thymine]], and [[guanine]]. Each can readily form [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonds]] to one of the others--A with T, C with G--so that two matching strands of DNA will group together in a double helix. The beginning of the strand is called 3' and the end 5'; these numbers refer to carbons in deoxyribose. The 3' end of one strand is next to the 5' end of the other; such an arrangement is called a [[crab canon]]. This form is called B-DNA; there are a few others. Reasons why [[thymine]] is used in DNA, and [[uracil]] in [[RNA]] need some explanation. Thymine is created from uracil in a [[methylation]] process, which is energetically expensive. In other words, uracil is cheaper for a cell that thymine. Unfortunately [[cytosine]] becomes [[deamination|deaminated]] with significant speed and becomes [[uracil]]. If uracil were used in DNA, that would be source of many mutations. With thymine, all uracils created in such way can be removed, and the proper fragment of DNA can be copied from the other strand. Cheaper uracil is used in RNA because RNA doesn't need to last a long time anyway. DNA does not code for any proteins directly. Instead, matching strands of messenger [[RNA]] (mRNA) are formed by [[enzyme|enzymes]] called [[RNA polymerase|RNA polymerases]]--this is called "transcription"--which are sent off into the [[cytoplasm]] for translation in the [[ribosome|ribosomes]]. Other kinds of RNA are also formed to help in the process--tRNA, rRNA, and snRNA. The [[genetic code]] used by these molecules to encode the sequence of proteins is highly conserved, nearly universally so. This suggests that all life on [[Earth]] is descended from a single original strain of living matter. In bacteria there is a single main strand of DNA, copied over when the cell replicates. There may also be small pieces of circular DNA called [[plasmid|plasmids]] floating in the cytoplasm that can be exchanged between cells. In [[eukaryote|eukaryotes]] there are typically multiple pieces of DNA contained in a central [[cell nucleus|nucleus]], separated out in cell division by a process called [[mitosis]]. There may be a single copy of the [[genome]] (whence the organism is [[haploid]]), two copies ([[diploid]]), or many copies ([[polyploid]]). In preparation for [[Sexual Reproduction|sexual reproduction]], haploid cells are produced from a diploid precursor in a process called [[meiosis]]. Eventually some haploid cells unite together to form a new diploid cell ([[syngamy]]) with half its genome from each diploid ancestor. When there are different versions of the same gene, their effects may be blended or a single version may be dominant. See also: [[Ploidy]], [[Biology]], [[Genetics]]. The discovery of DNA
The structure of nucleic acids was discovered in the C19. Later it was found that the sugar in nucleic acid could be either ribose ([[RNA]]) or deoxyribose (DNA). In 1943, [[Oswald Theodore Avery]] proved that DNA carries genetic information and suggested DNA might actually be the gene. In 1948, [[Linus Pauling]] discovered that many proteins take the shape of an alpha helix. In 1950, biochemist [[Erwin Chargaff]] found that the arrangement of bases in DNA varied, but the amount of certain bases always occurred in a one-to-one ratio. In England two separate investigations into DNA took place in the 1950s. At [[Cambridge University]], [[Francis Crick]] and [[James Watson]] were making physical models to eventually create an accurate picture of the molecule. At [[Kings College]] in [[London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining X-ray diffraction images of DNA. In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date. She had found that DNA can exist in two forms. From this she had deduced that the phosphate part of the molecule was on the outside. Based on an imperfect recollection of her information, Watson and Crick made a failed model. Franklin had also found that the X-ray diffractions of one of the forms of DNA had the characteristics of a helix. She suspected that DNA was helical but did not announce this. Watson and Crick made a crucial step, suggesting the molecule was made of two nucleotide chains, each in a helix as Franklin had found, but one going up and the other going down. Crick learned of the work of Chargaff in 1952. He added that to the model, so that matching base pairs interlocked to keep the distance between the chains constant. This model showed that each strand of the DNA molecule was a template for the other. The fit with experimental data was such that it was almost immediately accepted. Their paper 'A structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid' was published in April [[1953]]. further research uncovered how DNa made proteins in 1957. In [[1962]], Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the [[Nobel Prize]] for physiology/medicine, Franklin had died.

'''Deoxyribonucleic acid''' ('''DNA''') is the molecule in living things that contains the coding information for creating [[protein|proteins]]. It is also the molecule of heredity--whenever an organism reproduces, each offspring gets a copy of its parents' DNA. Structurally, nucleic acids are giant [[polymer|polymers]] composed of units called [[nucleotide|nucleotides]], each of which consists of a [[sugar]], a [[phosphate]], and a [[base]]. The sugar in DNA is [[deoxyribose]]. The four bases are [[adenine]], [[cytosine]], [[thymine]], and [[guanine]]. Each can readily form [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonds]] to one of the others--A with T, C with G--so that two matching strands of DNA will group together in a double helix. The beginning of the strand is called 3' and the end 5'; these numbers refer to carbons in deoxyribose. The 3' end of one strand is next to the 5' end of the other; such an arrangement is called a [[crab canon]]. This form is called B-DNA; there are a few others. The reason why thymine is used in DNA and [[uracil]] in [[RNA]] needs some explanation. Thymine is created from uracil in a [[methylation]] process, which is energetically expensive. In other words, uracil is cheaper for a cell than thymine. Unfortunately cytosine becomes [[deamination|deaminated]] with significant ease to become uracil. If uracil were used in DNA it result in many mutations going uncorrected, as there would be no way to tell the difference between a uracil and a deaminated cytosine. By using thymine in the genetic code instead of uracil, all uracils created in such way can be removed and replaced with cytosine using the guanine base on the opposite strand of DNA as a template. The cheaper uracil is used in RNA because RNA doesn't need to last a long time anyway. DNA does not code for any proteins directly. Instead, matching strands of messenger [[RNA]] (mRNA) are formed by [[enzyme|enzymes]] called [[RNA polymerase|RNA polymerases]]--this is called "transcription"--which are sent off into the [[cytoplasm]] for translation in the [[ribosome|ribosomes]]. Other kinds of RNA are also formed to help in the process--tRNA, rRNA, and snRNA. The [[genetic code]] used by these molecules to encode the sequence of proteins is highly conserved, nearly universally so. This suggests that all life on [[Earth]] is descended from a single original strain of living matter. In bacteria there is a single main strand of DNA, copied over when the cell replicates. There may also be small pieces of circular DNA called [[plasmid|plasmids]] floating in the cytoplasm that can be exchanged between cells. In [[eukaryote|eukaryotes]] there are typically multiple pieces of DNA contained in a central [[cell nucleus|nucleus]], separated out in cell division by a process called [[mitosis]]. There may be a single copy of the [[genome]] (whence the organism is [[haploid]]), two copies ([[diploid]]), or many copies ([[polyploid]]). In preparation for [[sexual reproduction]], haploid cells are produced from a diploid precursor in a process called [[meiosis]]. Eventually some haploid cells unite together to form a new diploid cell ([[syngamy]]) with half its genome from each diploid ancestor. When there are different versions of the same gene, their effects may be blended or a single version may be dominant. See also: [[Ploidy]], [[Biology]], [[Genetics]]. ===The discovery of DNA=== The structure of nucleic acids was discovered in the C19. Later it was found that the sugar in nucleic acid could be either ribose ([[RNA]]) or deoxyribose (DNA). In 1943, [[Oswald Theodore Avery]] proved that DNA carries genetic information and suggested DNA might actually be the gene. In 1948, [[Linus Pauling]] discovered that many proteins take the shape of an alpha helix. In 1950, biochemist [[Erwin Chargaff]] found that the arrangement of bases in DNA varied, but the amount of certain bases always occurred in a one-to-one ratio. In England two separate investigations into DNA took place in the 1950s. At [[Cambridge University]], [[Francis Crick]] and [[James Watson]] were making physical models to eventually create an accurate picture of the molecule. At [[Kings College]] in [[London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining X-ray diffraction images of DNA. In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date. She had found that DNA can exist in two forms. From this she had deduced that the phosphate part of the molecule was on the outside. Based on an imperfect recollection of her information, Watson and Crick made a failed model. Franklin had also found that the X-ray diffractions of one of the forms of DNA had the characteristics of a helix. She suspected that DNA was helical but did not announce this. Watson and Crick made a crucial step, suggesting the molecule was made of two nucleotide chains, each in a helix as Franklin had found, but one going up and the other going down. Crick learned of the work of Chargaff in 1952. He added that to the model, so that matching base pairs interlocked to keep the distance between the chains constant. Phosphorous is in yellow, showing the sugar-phosphate backbone with the dark blue of the nucleotide bases in the core of the helix. The major and minor grooves of the DNA helix are readily apparent.''

The sugar in DNA is [[deoxyribose]]. The four bases are [[adenine]], [[cytosine]], [[thymine]], and [[guanine]]. Each can readily form [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonds]] to one of the others--A with T, C with G--so that two matching strands of DNA will group together in a double helix. The beginning of the strand is called 3' and the end 5'; these numbers refer to carbons in deoxyribose. The 3' end of one strand is next to the 5' end of the other; such an arrangement is called a [[crab canon]]. This form is called B-DNA; there are a few others. The reason why thymine is used in DNA and [[uracil]] in [[RNA]] needs some explanation. Thymine is created from uracil in a [[methylation]] process, which is energetically expensive. In other words, uracil is cheaper for a cell than thymine. Unfortunately cytosine becomes [[deamination|deaminated]] with significant ease to become uracil. If uracil were used in DNA, it would result in many mutations going uncorrected, as there would be no way to tell the difference between a uracil and a deaminated cytosine. By using thymine in the genetic code instead of uracil, all uracils created in such way can be removed and replaced with cytosine using the guanine base on the opposite strand of DNA as a template. The cheaper uracil is used in RNA because RNA doesn't need to last a long time anyway. DNA does not code for any proteins directly. Instead, matching strands of messenger [[RNA]] (mRNA) are formed by [[enzyme|enzymes]] called [[RNA polymerase|RNA polymerases]]--this is called "transcription"--which are sent off into the [[cytoplasm]] for translation in the [[ribosome|ribosomes]]. Other kinds of RNA are also formed to help in the process--tRNA, rRNA, and snRNA. The [[genetic code]] used by these molecules to encode the sequence of proteins is highly conserved, nearly universally so. This suggests that all life on [[Earth]] is descended from a single original strain of living matter. In bacteria there is a single main strand of DNA, copied over when the cell replicates. There may also be small pieces of circular DNA called [[plasmid|plasmids]] floating in the cytoplasm that can be exchanged between cells. In [[eukaryote|eukaryotes]] there are typically multiple pieces of DNA contained in a central [[cell nucleus|nucleus]], separated out in cell division by a process called [[mitosis]]. There may be a single copy of the [[genome]] (whence the organism is [[haploid]]), two copies ([[diploid]]), or many copies ([[polyploid]]). In preparation for [[sexual reproduction]], haploid cells are produced from a diploid precursor in a process called [[meiosis]]. Eventually some haploid cells unite together to form a new diploid cell ([[syngamy]]) with half its genome from each diploid ancestor. When there are different versions of the same gene, their effects may be blended or a single version may be dominant. See also: [[Ploidy]], [[Biology]], [[Genetics]], [[DNA testing]], [[DNA vaccination]], [[selfish DNA]], [[junk DNA]], [[intron DNA]], [[DNA replication]], [[DNA chip]], [[DNA virus]], [[mitochondrial DNA]] ===The discovery of DNA=== The structure of nucleic acids was discovered in the C19. Later it was found that the sugar in nucleic acid could be either ribose ([[RNA]]) or deoxyribose (DNA). In 1943, [[Oswald Theodore Avery]] proved that DNA carries genetic information and suggested DNA might actually be the gene. In 1948, [[Linus Pauling]] discovered that many proteins take the shape of an alpha helix. In 1950, biochemist [[Erwin Chargaff]] found that the arrangement of bases in DNA varied, but the amount of certain bases always occurred in a one-to-one ratio. In England two separate investigations into DNA took place in the 1950s. At [[Cambridge University]], [[Francis Crick]] and [[James Watson]] were making physical models to eventually create an accurate picture of the molecule. At [[Kings College]] in [[London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining X-ray diffraction images of DNA. In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date. She had found that DNA can exist in two forms. From this she had deduced that the phosphate part of the molecule was on the outside. Based on an imperfect recollection of her information, Watson and Crick made a failed model. Franklin had also found that the X-ray diffractions of one of the forms of DNA had the characteristics of a helix. She suspected that DNA was helical but did not announce this. Watson and Crick made a crucial step, suggesting the molecule was made of two nucleotide chains, each in a helix as Franklin had found, but one going up and the other going down. Crick learned of the work of Chargaff in 1952. He added that to the model, so that matching base pairs interlocked to keep the distance between the chains constant. This model showed that each strand of the DNA molecule was a template for the other. The fit with experimental data was such that it was almost immediately accepted. Their paper 'A structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid' was published in April [[1953]]. further research uncovered how DNa made proteins in 1957. In [[1962]], Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the [[Nobel Prize]] for physiology/medicine, Franklin had died. In [[1988]], Watson was appointed as the Head of the [[Human Genome Project]] at the [[National Institutes of Health]]. [[Image:Dna-helix.png]] ''Space-filling model of a section of DNA molecule. Phosphorus is in yellow, showing the sugar-phosphate backbone with the dark blue of the nucleotide bases in the core of the helix. The major and minor grooves of the DNA helix are readily apparent.''

The reason why thymine is used in DNA and [[uracil]] in [[RNA]] needs some explanation. Thymine is created from uracil in a [[methylation]] process, which is energetically expensive. In other words, uracil is cheaper for a cell than thymine. Unfortunately cytosine becomes [[deamination|deaminated]] with significant ease to become uracil. If uracil were used in DNA, it would result in many mutations going uncorrected, as there would be no way to tell the difference between a uracil and a deaminated cytosine. By using thymine in the genetic code instead of uracil, all uracils created in such way can be removed and replaced with cytosine using the guanine base on the opposite strand of DNA as a template. The cheaper uracil is used in RNA because RNA doesn't need to last a long time anyway. DNA does not code for any proteins directly. Instead, matching strands of messenger [[RNA]] (mRNA) are formed by [[enzyme]]s called [[RNA polymerase]]s--this is called [[transcription]]--which are sent off into the [[cytoplasm]] for [[translation]] in the [[ribosome|ribosomes]]. Other kinds of RNA are also formed to help in the process--tRNA, rRNA, and snRNA. The [[genetic code]] used by these molecules to encode the sequence of proteins is highly conserved, nearly universally so. This suggests that all life on [[Earth]] is descended from a single original strain of living matter. In bacteria there is a single main double-stranded DNA molecule (often but not always circular), copied over when the cell replicates. There may also be small pieces of circular DNA called [[plasmid]]s floating in the cytoplasm that can be exchanged between cells. In [[eukaryote]]s there are typically multiple pieces of DNA contained in a central [[cell nucleus|nucleus]], separated out in cell division by a process called [[mitosis]]. There may be a single copy of the [[genome]] (whence the organism is [[haploid]]), two copies ([[diploid]]), or many copies ([[polyploid]]). In preparation for [[sexual reproduction]], haploid cells are produced from a diploid precursor in a process called [[meiosis]]. Eventually some haploid cells unite together to form a new diploid cell ([[syngamy]]) with half its genome from each diploid ancestor. When there are different versions of the same gene, their effects may be blended or a single version may be dominant. See also: [[Ploidy]], [[Biology]], [[Genetics]], [[DNA testing]], [[DNA vaccination]], [[selfish DNA]], [[junk DNA]], [[intron DNA]], [[DNA replication]], [[DNA chip]], [[DNA virus]], [[mitochondrial DNA]], [[LUCA]] ==The discovery of DNA== The structure of nucleic acids was discovered in the C19. Later it was found that the sugar in nucleic acid could be either ribose ([[RNA]]) or deoxyribose (DNA). In 1943, [[Oswald Theodore Avery]] proved that DNA carries genetic information and suggested DNA might actually be the [[gene]]. In 1948, [[Linus Pauling]] discovered that many proteins take the shape of an alpha helix. In 1950, biochemist [[Erwin Chargaff]] found that the arrangement of bases in DNA varied, but the amount of certain bases always occurred in a one-to-one ratio. In England two separate investigations into DNA took place in the 1950s. At [[Cambridge University]], '''[[Francis Crick]] and [[James Watson]]''' were making physical models to eventually create an accurate picture of the molecule. At [[King's College, London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining X-ray diffraction images of DNA.
[[Image:Dna-helix.png]]
In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date. She had found that DNA can exist in two forms. From this she had deduced that the phosphate part of the molecule was on the outside. Based on an imperfect recollection of her information, Watson and Crick made a failed model. Franklin had also found that the [[crystallography|X-ray diffraction pattern]] of one of the forms of DNA suggested a helix. She suspected that DNA was helical but did not announce this. Watson and Crick made a crucial step, suggesting the molecule was made of two nucleotide chains, each in a helix as Franklin had found, but one going up and the other going down. Crick learned of the work of Chargaff in 1952. He added that to the model, so that matching base pairs interlocked to keep the distance between the chains constant. This model showed that each strand of the DNA molecule was a template for the other. The fit with experimental data was such that it was almost immediately accepted. Their paper [http://www.nature.com/genomics/human/watson-crick/ 'A structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid'] was published on [[April 25]], [[1953]] but the structure of DNA had been uncovered two months earlier on [[February 21]] (the first announcement wasn't made until [[February 28]]). Further research uncovered how DNA made proteins in 1957. Molecular biology was born. In [[1962]], Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the [[Nobel Prize]] for physiology/medicine, Franklin had died. In [[1988]], Watson was appointed as the Head of the [[Human Genome Project]] at the [[National Institutes of Health]]. ==External addresses== *Google: [http://directory.google.com/Top/Science/Biology/Biochemistry_and_Molecular_Biology/Biomolecules/Nucleic_Acids/ Nucleic Acids] *[http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/sci/tech/2949629.stm 17 April, 2003, BBC News: Most ancient DNA ever?]

[[da:DNA]][[de:Desoxyribonukleinsäure]] [[eo:DNA]] [[es:ADN]] [[fr:ADN]] [[pl:DNA]][[nl:DNA]][[ja:DNA]]
[[image:dna-split.png]]
''DNA replication
(image from [http://www.ornl.gov/hgmis DOE Human Genome project])''
'''Deoxyribonucleic acid''' ('''DNA''') is the primary chemical component of [[chromosome]]s and the material of which [[gene]]s are made. It is sometimes called the "molecule of heredity," because parents transmit copied portions of their own DNA to offspring during [[reproduction]], and because by doing so they propogate their traits. In fact, the units of DNA that reside in the [[nucleus]] of [[eukaryote|eukaryotic]] [[biological cell|cells]], and DNA pieces as people typically think of them, are not single molecules. Strictly speaking, they are ''pairs'' of vine-like molecules ([[polymer]]s), which entwine in the shape of a '''double helix''' (top half of the illustration at the right). Nucleic-acid [[polymer|polymers]] are composed of chemically linked sequences of [[nucleotide|nucleotides]], each consisting of a [[deoxyribose]] [[sugar]], a [[phosphate]], and a [[base]]. There are four kinds of nucleotide in DNA, which are commonly referred to by the identity of the bases that distinguish them. These are [[adenine]]('''A'''), [[thymine]]('''T'''), [[cytosine]]('''C''') and [[guanine]]('''G'''). In the double-helical form of DNA, two polemeric strands come together through a complementary pairing of bases. Each base forms [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonds]] readily to only one other--'''A''' to '''T''' and '''C''' to '''G'''--so that the identity of the base on one strand dictates what base must face it on the opposing strand, if the two are to properly mesh. Thus the entire nucleotide sequence of each strand is complementary to the other, and when separated each may act as a template with which to [[replication|replicate]] the other from loose nucleotides (bottom half of the illustration at the right). Because, hydrogen bonds are weak (compared to true chemical bonds, for example), the strands of the double helix can be easily separated by enzymes or even, as in the case of [[PCR]], by gentle heating. On the other hand, gentle heating works only on pieces of DNA that are less than about 10,000 base-pairs ("bp") long. The intertwining of the DNA strands makes long segments difficult to separate. Enzymes knowns as [[helicase]]s unwind the strands to facilitate the advance of sequence-reading enzymes such as [[DNA polymerase]]. The unwinding requires that helicases chemically cleave the phosphate "backbone" of one the strands, so that it may swivel around the other. When a piece of helical DNA is closed in a circle, as in [[plasmid]] DNA, its strands are [[topology|topologically]] knotted. They cannot be separated by gentle heating or by any process that does not involve the breaking of a strand. The task of unknotting topologically linked strands of DNA falls to enzymes known as "[[topoisomerase]]s". Some of these enzymes unknot circular DNA by cleaving two strands so that another double-stranded segment can pass through. Unknotting is required for the replication of circular DNA as well as for various types of [[recombination]] in linear DNA. Because nucleotides are assymetric, DNA and RNA sequences have a polarity or directionality. For reasons of chemical nomenclature, the two termini of a strand are referred to as the 5' and the 3' ends (pronounced "five prime" and "three prime"). Biologists and enzymes alike read nucleotide sequences always in the direction from 5' to 3'. A 5'-to-3' sequence that is translated into protein is said to be "sense," while the sequence of the complimentary strand is called "antisense." Consequently, the DNA strand that acts as template for transcription is the antisense strand, even though the resulting RNA transcript is a copy of the sense strand. The fact that the 3' end of one DNA strand flanks the 5' end of the other makes the arrangement a [[crab canon]]. The reason why thymine is used in DNA and [[uracil]] in [[RNA]] needs some explanation. Thymine is created from uracil in a [[methylation]] process, which is energetically expensive. In other words, uracil is cheaper for a cell than thymine. Unfortunately cytosine becomes [[deamination|deaminated]] with significant ease to become uracil. If uracil were used in DNA, it would result in many mutations going uncorrected, as there would be no way to tell the difference between a uracil and a deaminated cytosine. By using thymine in the genetic code instead of uracil, all uracils created in such way can be removed and replaced with cytosine using the guanine base on the opposite strand of DNA as a template. The cheaper uracil is used in RNA because RNA doesn't need to last a long time anyway. The DNA of genes does not code for any proteins directly. Instead, matching strands of messenger [[RNA]] (mRNA) are formed by [[enzyme]]s called [[RNA polymerase]]s--this is called [[transcription]]--which are sent off into the [[cytoplasm]] for [[translation]] in the [[ribosome|ribosomes]]. Other kinds of RNA are also formed to help in the process--tRNA, rRNA, and snRNA. The [[genetic code]] used by these molecules to encode the sequence of proteins is highly conserved, nearly universally so. This suggests that all life on [[Earth]] is descended from a single original strain of living matter. In bacteria there is a single main double-stranded DNA molecule (often but not always circular), copied over when the cell replicates. There may also be small pieces of circular DNA called [[plasmid]]s floating in the cytoplasm that can be exchanged between cells. In [[eukaryote]]s there are typically multiple pieces of DNA contained in a central [[cell nucleus|nucleus]], separated out in cell division by a process called [[mitosis]]. There may be a single copy of the [[genome]] (whence the organism is [[haploid]]), two copies ([[diploid]]), or many copies ([[polyploid]]). In preparation for [[sexual reproduction]], haploid cells are produced from a diploid precursor in a process called [[meiosis]]. Eventually some haploid cells unite together to form a new diploid cell ([[syngamy]]) with half its genome from each diploid ancestor. When there are different versions of the same gene, their effects may be blended or a single version may be dominant. See also: [[Ploidy]], [[Biology]], [[Genetics]], [[DNA testing]], [[DNA vaccination]], [[selfish DNA]], [[junk DNA]], [[intron DNA]], [[DNA replication]], [[DNA chip]], [[DNA virus]], [[mitochondrial DNA]], [[LUCA]] ==The discovery of DNA and its structure== The chemical composition of nucleotides and the polymeric nature of nucleic acids was discovered in the 19the century. Later it was found that the sugar could be either ribose ([[RNA]]) or deoxyribose (DNA). In 1943, [[Oswald Theodore Avery]] proved that DNA carries genetic information and suggested DNA might actually be the [[gene]]. In 1948, [[Linus Pauling]] discovered that many proteins take the shape of an alpha helix. In 1950, biochemist [[Erwin Chargaff]] found that the exact amount of the different nucleotides varied from one DNA sample to another, but that always adenine equaled thymine in abundance, and guanine equaled cytosine. In England two separate investigations into DNA took place in the 1950s. At [[Cambridge University]], '''[[Francis Crick]] and [[James Watson]]''' were making physical models to eventually create an accurate picture of the molecule. At [[King's College, London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining X-ray diffraction images of DNA.
[[Image:Dna-helix.png]]
In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date. She had found that DNA can exist in two forms. From this she had deduced that the phosphate part of the molecule was on the outside. Based on an imperfect recollection of her information, Watson and Crick made a failed model. Franklin had also found that the [[crystallography|X-ray diffraction pattern]] of one of the forms of DNA suggested a helix. She suspected that DNA was helical but did not announce this. Watson and Crick made a crucial step, suggesting the molecule was made of two nucleotide chains, each in a helix as Franklin had found, but one going up and the other going down. Crick learned of the work of Chargaff in 1952. He added that to the model, so that matching base pairs interlocked to keep the distance between the chains constant. This model showed that each strand of the DNA molecule was a template for the other. The fit with experimental data was such that it was almost immediately accepted. Their paper [http://www.nature.com/genomics/human/watson-crick/ 'A structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid'] was published on [[April 25]], [[1953]] but the structure of DNA had been uncovered two months earlier on [[February 21]] (the first announcement wasn't made until [[February 28]]). Further research uncovered how DNA made proteins in 1957. Molecular biology was born. In [[1962]], Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the [[Nobel Prize]] for physiology/medicine, Franklin had died. In [[1988]], Watson was appointed as the Head of the [[Human Genome Project]] at the [[National Institutes of Health]]. ==External addresses== *Google: [http://directory.google.com/Top/Science/Biology/Biochemistry_and_Molecular_Biology/Biomolecules/Nucleic_Acids/ Nucleic Acids] *[http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/sci/tech/2949629.stm 17 April, 2003, BBC News: Most ancient DNA ever?]

[[da:DNA]][[de:Desoxyribonukleinsäure]] [[eo:DNA]] [[es:ADN]] [[fr:ADN]] [[pl:DNA]][[nl:DNA]][[ja:DNA]]
[[image:dna-split.png]]
''DNA replication
(image from [http://www.ornl.gov/hgmis DOE Human Genome project])''
'''Deoxyribonucleic acid''' ('''DNA''') is the primary chemical component of [[chromosome]]s and the material of which [[gene]]s are made. It is sometimes called the "molecule of heredity," because parents transmit copied portions of their own DNA to offspring during [[reproduction]], and because in doing so they propogate their traits. In fact, the units of DNA that reside in the [[nucleus]] of [[eukaryote|eukaryotic]] [[biological cell|cells]], and DNA pieces as people typically think of them, are not single molecules. Rather they are ''pairs'' of long molecules that entwine like vines to form a '''double helix''' (top half of the illustration at the right). Each vine-like molecule, or DNA ''strand'', is a chemically linked sequence of [[nucleotide|nucleotides]], each consisting of a [[deoxyribose]] [[sugar]], a [[phosphate]], and a [[base]]. DNA strands are thus considered [[polymer]]s. There are four kinds of nucleotide in DNA, which are commonly referred to by the identity of the bases that distinguish them. These are [[adenine]]('''A'''), [[thymine]]('''T'''), [[cytosine]]('''C''') and [[guanine]]('''G'''). In the double-helical form of DNA, two polemeric strands come together through a complementary pairing of bases. Each base forms [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonds]] readily to only one other--'''A''' to '''T''' and '''C''' to '''G'''--so that the identity of the base on one strand dictates what base must face it on the opposing strand, if the two are to properly mesh. Thus the entire nucleotide sequence of each strand is complementary to the other, and when separated each may act as a template with which to [[replication|replicate]] the other from loose nucleotides (middle and lower half of the illustration at the right). ==Mechanical Properties Relevant to Biology== Because, hydrogen bonds are weak (compared to true chemical bonds, for example), the strands of the double helix can be easily separated by enzymes or even, as in the case of [[PCR]], by gentle heating. On the other hand, gentle heating works only on pieces of DNA that are less than about 10,000 base-pairs ("bp") long. The intertwining of the DNA strands makes long segments difficult to separate. Enzymes knowns as [[helicase]]s unwind the strands to facilitate the advance of sequence-reading enzymes such as [[DNA polymerase]]. The unwinding requires that helicases chemically cleave the phosphate "backbone" of one the strands, so that it may swivel around the other. When a piece of helical DNA is closed in a circle, as in [[plasmid]] DNA, its strands are [[topology|topologically]] knotted. They cannot be separated by gentle heating or by any process that does not involve the breaking of a strand. The task of unknotting topologically linked strands of DNA falls to enzymes known as "[[topoisomerase]]s". Some of these enzymes unknot circular DNA by cleaving two strands so that another double-stranded segment can pass through. Unknotting is required for the replication of circular DNA as well as for various types of [[recombination]] in linear DNA. In fibers or crystals the DNA helix can assume two forms, the "B" and the "Z", which differ only slightly in their geometry and dimensions. The B form appears to be the only biologically relevant one. It is 2 nanometers wide and extends 3.4 nm per ten base pairs (bp) of sequence, which is also the approximate length of sequence in each turn about the helical axis. The width of the double helix makes it too slender even to detect by conventional [[transmission electron microscope|electron microscopy]]. Yet the molecules can be "macroscopic" in length--approximately 5 centimeters, for the DNA segments in a human chromosome. Consequently, cells must compact or "package" DNA to carry it within them. This is in effect one of the functions of the chromosomes, which contain spool-like [[protein]]s known[[histone]]s, around which the DNA is wound. B DNA turns 360 degrees per 10.6 bp when it is under no strain. But many biological processes can lead to strain. Increases and decreases in helical twisting care referred to as positive and negative [[supercoil|"supercoiling"]]. ==DNA Sequence Reading== Because nucleotides are assymetric, DNA and RNA sequences have a polarity or directionality. For reasons of chemical nomenclature, the two termini of a strand are referred to as the 5' and the 3' ends (pronounced "five prime" and "three prime"). Biologists and enzymes alike read nucleotide sequences always in the direction from 5' to 3'. A 5'-to-3' sequence that is translated into protein is said to be "sense," while the sequence of the complimentary strand is called "antisense." Consequently, the DNA strand that acts as template for transcription is the antisense strand, even though the resulting RNA transcript is a copy of the sense strand. The fact that the 3' end of one DNA strand flanks the 5' end of the other makes the arrangement a [[crab canon]]. ==The discovery of DNA and its structure== The chemical composition of nucleotides and the polymeric nature of nucleic acids was discovered in the 19the century. Later it was found that the sugar could be either ribose ([[RNA]]) or deoxyribose (DNA). In 1943, [[Oswald Theodore Avery]] proved that DNA carries genetic information and suggested DNA might actually be the [[gene]]. In 1948, [[Linus Pauling]] discovered that many proteins take the shape of an alpha helix. In 1950, biochemist [[Erwin Chargaff]] found that the exact amount of the different nucleotides varied from one DNA sample to another, but that always adenine equaled thymine in abundance, and guanine equaled cytosine. In England two separate investigations into DNA took place in the 1950s. At [[Cambridge University]], '''[[Francis Crick]] and [[James Watson]]''' were making physical models to eventually create an accurate picture of the molecule. At [[King's College, London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining X-ray diffraction images of DNA.
[[Image:Dna-helix.png]]
In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date. She had found that DNA can exist in two forms. From this she had deduced that the phosphate part of the molecule was on the outside. Based on an imperfect recollection of her information, Watson and Crick made a failed model. Franklin had also found that the [[crystallography|X-ray diffraction pattern]] of one of the forms of DNA suggested a helix. She suspected that DNA was helical but did not announce this. Watson and Crick made a crucial step, suggesting the molecule was made of two nucleotide chains, each in a helix as Franklin had found, but one going up and the other going down. Crick learned of the work of Chargaff in 1952. He added that to the model, so that matching base pairs interlocked to keep the distance between the chains constant. This model showed that each strand of the DNA molecule was a template for the other. The fit with experimental data was such that it was almost immediately accepted. Their paper [http://www.nature.com/genomics/human/watson-crick/ 'A structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid'] was published on [[April 25]], [[1953]] but the structure of DNA had been uncovered two months earlier on [[February 21]] (the first announcement wasn't made until [[February 28]]). Further research uncovered how DNA made proteins in 1957. Molecular biology was born. In [[1962]], Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the [[Nobel Prize]] for physiology/medicine, Franklin had died. In [[1988]], Watson was appointed as the Head of the [[Human Genome Project]] at the [[National Institutes of Health]]. ==External addresses== *Google: [http://directory.google.com/Top/Science/Biology/Biochemistry_and_Molecular_Biology/Biomolecules/Nucleic_Acids/ Nucleic Acids] *[http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/sci/tech/2949629.stm 17 April, 2003, BBC News: Most ancient DNA ever?]

''DNA replication
(image from [http://www.ornl.gov/hgmis DOE Human Genome project])''

Rather they are ''pairs'' of long molecules that entwine like vines to form a '''double helix''' (top half of the illustration at the right). Each vine-like molecule, or DNA ''strand'', is a chemically linked sequence of [[nucleotide|nucleotides]], each consisting of a [[deoxyribose]] [[sugar]], a [[phosphate]], and a [[base]]. These are [[adenine]]('''A'''), [[thymine]]('''T'''), [[cytosine]]('''C''') and [[guanine]]('''G'''). In the double-helical form of DNA, two polemeric strands come together through a complementary pairing of bases. Each base forms [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonds]] readily to only one other--'''A''' to '''T''' and '''C''' to '''G'''--so that the identity of the base on one strand dictates what base must face it on the opposing strand, if the two are to properly mesh. Thus the entire nucleotide sequence of each strand is complementary to the other, and when separated each may act as a template with which to [[replication|replicate]] the other from loose nucleotides (middle and lower half of the illustration at the right). ==Mechanical Properties Relevant to Biology== Because, hydrogen bonds are weak (compared to true chemical bonds, for example), the strands of the double helix can be easily separated by enzymes or even, as in the case of [[PCR]], by gentle heating. On the other hand, gentle heating works only on pieces of DNA that are less than about 10,000 base-pairs ("bp") long. The intertwining of the DNA strands makes long segments difficult to separate. Enzymes knowns as [[helicase]]s unwind the strands to facilitate the advance of sequence-reading enzymes such as [[DNA polymerase]]. The unwinding requires that helicases chemically cleave the phosphate "backbone" of one the strands, so that it may swivel around the other. When a piece of helical DNA is closed in a circle, as in [[plasmid]] DNA, its strands are [[topology|topologically]] knotted. They cannot be separated by gentle heating or by any process that does not involve the breaking of a strand. The task of unknotting topologically linked strands of DNA falls to enzymes known as "[[topoisomerase]]s". Some of these enzymes unknot circular DNA by cleaving two strands so that another double-stranded segment can pass through. Unknotting is required for the replication of circular DNA as well as for various types of [[recombination]] in linear DNA. In fibers or crystals the DNA helix can assume two forms, the "B" and the "Z", which differ only slightly in their geometry and dimensions. The B form appears to be the only biologically relevant one. It is 2 nanometers wide and extends 3.4 nm per ten base pairs (bp) of sequence, which is also the approximate length of sequence in each turn about the helical axis. The width of the double helix makes it too slender even to detect by conventional [[transmission electron microscope|electron microscopy]]. Yet the molecules can be "macroscopic" in length--approximately 5 centimeters, for the DNA segments in a human chromosome. Consequently, cells must compact or "package" DNA to carry it within them. This is in effect one of the functions of the chromosomes, which contain spool-like [[protein]]s known[[histone]]s, around which the DNA is wound. B DNA turns 360 degrees per 10.6 bp when it is under no strain. But many biological processes can lead to strain. Increases and decreases in helical twisting care referred to as positive and negative [[supercoil|"supercoiling"]]. ==DNA Sequence Reading== The assymetric shape and linkages between nucleotides give DNA strands a polarity or "directionality." For reasons of chemical nomenclature, those who work with DNA refer to the two termini of a strand as the 5' and the 3' ends (pronounced "five prime" and "three prime"). DNA workers and enzymes alike read nucleotide sequences always in the direction from 5' to 3'. A 5'-to-3' sequence that is translated into protein is said to be "sense," while the sequence of the complimentary strand is called "antisense." Consequently, the DNA strand that acts as template for transcription is the antisense strand, even though the resulting RNA transcript is a copy of the sense strand. The fact that the 3' end of one DNA strand flanks the 5' end of the other makes the arrangement a [[crab canon]]. ==The Discovery of DNA and its Structure== Early biochemists first isolated DNA and [[RNA]] together from cell nuclei. Researchers appreciated the polymeric nature of their "nucleic acid" isolates before they realized that nucleotides were of two types--one containing ribose and the other deoxyribose. Completed mostly in the 19th century, this work led to the identification and naming of DNA as a substance that is distinct from RNA. Finally in 1943, [[Oswald Theodore Avery]] proved that DNA could carry genetic information, suggesting that it might actually be the [[gene]]s. How this hypothesis could be true was unimaginable at the time. Because DNA was reducible to four nucleotides,its chemical composition appeared simple. Organisms, on the other hand, are fantastically complex. The idea that information could exist in a chemical in the same way it exists in text--as a finite alphabet of letters arranged in a sequence of unlimited length--emerged only after discovery of DNA's structure. Indeed, few researchers imagined that the structure of DNA had much to say at all about genetics, and only a few groups made it their goal to discover it. These included an American group led by [[Linus Pauling]], and two groups in England. At [[Cambridge University]], '''[[Francis Crick]]''' and '''[[James Watson]]''' were building physical models with balls and sticks, which incorporated the known chemical structures of the nucleotides and the known identities of the nucleotide atoms through which one nucleotide fastened to the next in the polymer. At [[King's College, London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining [[crystallography|X-ray diffraction]] patterns from DNA fibers. All of this work too place in the 1950s. A key inspiration in this work was the discovery in [[1948]] by Pauling that a helix (see [[alpha helix]]) was a shape assumed by many proteins. Pauling had deduced the structure from X ray patterns. The intitial crude diffraction data from DNA also suggested helices. Yet this insight fell far short of an explanation. There remained the questions of how many strands came together, whether this number was the same for every helix, whether the bases pointed in or out, and ultimately what were the explicit angles and coordingates of all the bonds and atoms. Such questions motivated the modeling efforts of Watson and Crick.
[[Image:Dna-helix.png]]
In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date. She had found that DNA can exist in two forms. From this she had deduced that the phosphate part of the molecule was on the outside. Based on an imperfect recollection of her information, Watson and Crick made a triple helical model, which failed to fit the data on later inspection. While Franklin's data clearly implied a helix and she appreciated this fact, she did not publish this conclusion. In their modelling, Watson and Crick limited themselves to what they saw as chemically and biologically reasonable. Still the breadth of possibilities was very wide. A breakthrough occured in 1952, when Crick learned of work that [[Erwin Chargaff]] had reported two years earlier. Chargaff observed that the relative proportions of the different nucleotides varied between one DNA sample and another, but that the proportions of adenine and thymine are always equal, and that the same was true of guanine and cytosine. Watson and Crick had begun to contemplate double helical arrangements, and they saw that by reversing the directionality of one strand to the other, they could provide an explanation for Chargaff's ratios. This explanation was the base pairing, which also had the effect of ensuring a fixed distance between the chains no matter the sequence. The fit of Watson and Crick's model with diffraction patterns and other experimental data was such that it was almost immediately accepted. Their paper [http://www.nature.com/genomics/human/watson-crick/ 'A structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid'] was published on [[April 25]], [[1953]] but the structure of DNA had been uncovered two months earlier on [[February 21]] (the first announcement wasn't made until [[February 28]]). Further research uncovered how DNA made proteins in 1957. Molecular biology was born. In [[1962]], Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the [[Nobel Prize]] for physiology/medicine, Franklin had died. In [[1988]], Watson was appointed as the Head of the [[Human Genome Project]] at the [[National Institutes of Health]]. [[da:DNA]][[de:Desoxyribonukleinsäure]] [[eo:DNA]] [[es:ADN]] [[fr:ADN]] [[pl:DNA]][[nl:DNA]][[ja:DNA]]
[[image:dna-split.png]]
''DNA replication
(image from [http://www.ornl.gov/hgmis DOE Human Genome project])''
'''Deoxyribonucleic acid''' ('''DNA''') is the primary chemical component of [[chromosome]]s and the material of which [[gene]]s are made. It is sometimes called the "molecule of heredity," because parents transmit copied portions of their own DNA to offspring during [[reproduction]], and because in doing so they propogate their traits. In fact, the units of DNA that reside in the [[nucleus]] of [[eukaryote|eukaryotic]] [[biological cell|cells]], and DNA pieces as people typically think of them, are not single molecules. Rather they are ''pairs'' of long molecules, which entwine like vines to form a '''double helix''' (top half of the illustration at the right). Each vine-like molecule, or ''strand'' of DNA, is a chemically linked chain of [[nucleotide|nucleotides]], which consist of a [[deoxyribose]] [[sugar]], a [[phosphate]], and one of four varieties of "[[aromatic]]" [[base]]. Because DNA strands are composed of these nucleotide subunits, they are considered [[polymer]]s. The diversity bases means that DNA consists of four distinct kinds of nucleotide, which commonly are referred to by the identity of their bases. These are [[adenine]]('''A'''), [[thymine]]('''T'''), [[cytosine]]('''C''') and [[guanine]]('''G'''). In a double helix, two polemeric strands come together through a complementary pairing of bases that occurs by [[hydrogen bond|hydrogen bonding]]. Each base forms hydrogen bonds readily to only one other--'''A''' to '''T''' and '''C''' to '''G'''--so that the identity of the base on one strand dictates what base must face it on the opposing strand, if the two are to properly mesh. Thus the entire nucleotide [[sequence]] of each strand is complementary to the other, and when separated each may act as a template with which to [[replication|replicate]] the other from loose nucleotides (middle and lower half of the illustration at the right). ==Mechanical Properties Relevant to Biology== Because, hydrogen bonds are weak compared to true chemical bonds, the strands of the double helix can be easily separated by enzymes or even, as in the case of [[PCR]], by gentle heating. On the other hand, gentle heating works only on pieces of DNA that are less than about 10,000 ''base pairs'' (10 kilobase pairs, or 10 kbp) long. The intertwining of the DNA strands makes long segments difficult to separate. Enzymes knowns as [[helicase]]s unwind the strands to facilitate the advance of sequence-reading enzymes such as [[DNA polymerase]]. The unwinding requires that helicases chemically cleave the phosphate "backbone" of one the strands, so that it may swivel around the other. When a piece of double-helical DNA is closed into a circle, as in [[plasmid]] DNA, the strands are [[knot theory|topologically]] knotted. This means they cannot be separated by gentle heating or by any process that does not involve the breaking of a strand. The task of unknotting topologically linked strands of DNA falls to enzymes known as "[[topoisomerase]]s". Some of these enzymes unknot circular DNA by cleaving two strands so that another double-stranded segment can pass through. Unknotting is required for the replication of circular DNA as well as for various types of [[recombination]] in linear DNA.
[[Image:Dna-helix.png]]
In fibers or crystals the DNA helix assumes either of two forms, the "B" and the "Z". These two differ only slightly in their geometry and dimensions, but the B form appears to be the only one of biological relevance. It is 2 [[nanometer]]s wide and extends 3.4 nanometers per ten bp of sequence. This is also the approximate length of sequence over which the helix makes one complete turn about its axis. The narrow breadth of the double helix makes it impossible to detect by conventional [[transmission electron microscope|electron microscopy]], except by heavy staining. At the same time, the pieces of DNA that appear in many cells can be "macroscopic" in length--approximately 5 centimeters, for the DNA molecules in a human chromosome. Consequently, cells must compact or "package" DNA to carry it inside them. This in effect is one of the functions of the chromosomes, which contain spool-like [[protein]]s known as [[histone]]s, around which DNA winds. The "B" DNA helix twists 360 degrees per 10.6 bp in the absence of strain. But many molecular biological processes can induce strain. A DNA segment with excessive or insufficient helical twisting is referred to, respectively, as either positively or negatively [[supercoil|"supercoiled"]]. ==DNA Sequence Reading== The assymetric shape and linkage of nucleotides give DNA strands an orientation or "directionality." For reasons of chemical nomenclature, people who work with DNA refer to the termini of a strand as the 5' and the 3' ends (pronounced "five prime" and "three prime"). DNA workers and enzymes alike read nucleotide sequences always in the 5'-to-3' direction. As a result, even if the sequences of opposing strands were identical and not merely complimentary, cells could properly translate a gene into a protein from one of the two strands, because the sequence of the other they could read only in reverse. But indeed the sequences of paired strands run not only in opposite direction but present complementary bases. Translated or translatable sequences are said to be "sense," while their complimentary sequences are said to be "antisense." Somewhat ironically, it follows then that it is the antisense strand that serves as the template for transcription. Reassuringly, however, the resulting [[RNA]] transcript is sense. The fact that the 3' end of one DNA strand flanks the 5' end of the other makes the arrangement a "[[crab canon]]." ==The Discovery of DNA and its Structure== Working in the 19th century, biochemists initially isolated DNA and [[RNA]] together from cell nuclei. They were relatively quick to appreciate the polymeric nature of their "nucleic acid" isolates, but realized only later that nucleotides were of two types--one containing ribose and the other deoxyribose. It was this subsequent discovery that led to the identification and naming of DNA as a substance distinct from RNA. It was not until 1943 that [[Oswald Theodore Avery]] provided compelling evidence that DNA could carry genetic information, thus suggesting that it might be the hypothesized [[gene]]s. How this hypothesis could be true was unimaginable at the time. Because DNA samples always reduced to the same four nucleotides, the chemical composition of DNA appeared simple; perhaps even uniform. Organisms, on the other hand, are fantastically complex individually and wildly diverse collectively. Geneticists did not speak of genes as conveyors of "information" in such words, but if they had, they would not have hesitated to quantify the amount of information that genes conveyed as vast. The idea that information might reside in a chemical in the same way that it exists in text--as a finite alphabet of letters arranged in a sequence of unlimited length--had not yet been conceived. It would emerge upon the discovery of DNA's structure, but few researchers imagined that the DNA's structure had much to say about genetics. In the 1950s, only a few groups made it their goal to determine the structure of DNA. These included an American group led by [[Linus Pauling]], and two in England. At [[Cambridge University]], [[Francis Crick]] and [[James Watson]] were building physical models using balls and sticks, in which they incorporated the known chemical structures of the nucleotides, as well as the known position of the linkages joining one nucleotide to the next along the polymer. At [[King's College, London]], [[Maurice Wilkins]] and [[Rosalind Franklin]] were examining [[crystallography|X-ray diffraction]] patterns from DNA fibers. A key inspiration in the work of all of these teams was the discovery in [[1948]] by Pauling that a helix (see [[alpha helix]]) was a shape that many proteins assumed. Pauling had deduced this structure from X ray patterns. Even in the intitial crude diffraction data from DNA, it was evident the structure involved helices. But this insight was only a beginning. There remained the questions of how many strands came together, whether this number was the same for every helix, whether the bases pointed toward the helical axis or away, and ultimately what were the explicit angles and coordingates of all the bonds and atoms. Such questions motivated the modeling efforts of Watson and Crick. In 1951, Watson attended a lecture by Franklin on her work to date, in which she displayed her diffraction patterns. She told her audience that DNA existed in two helical forms, and that the phosphates stood at the periphery of the helices. Based on an imperfect recollection of the patterns Franklin presented, Watson and Crick made a triple helical model, which failed to fit Franklin's data on later inspection. Franklin did not immediately publish her findings. In their modelling, Watson and Crick restricted themselves to what they saw as chemically and biologically reasonable. Still the breadth of possibilities was very wide. A breakthrough occured in 1952, when Crick learned of work that [[Erwin Chargaff]] had reported two years earlier. Chargaff had observed that the proportions of the four nucleotides vary between one DNA sample and the next, but that for particular pairs of nucleotides--adenine and thymine, guanine and cytosine--the two nucleotides are always present in equal proportion. Watson and Crick had begun to contemplate double helical arrangements, and they saw that by reversing the directionality of one strand to the other, they could provide an explanation for Chargaff's puzzling finding. This explanation was the complementary pairing of the bases, which also had the effect of ensuring that the distance between the phosphate chains did not vary along a sequence. The scientific community embraced Watson and Crick's model more or less immediately upon its presentation. Arriving at their conclusion on [[February 21]] [[1953]], Watson and Crick made their first informal announcements on [[February 28]]. Their paper [http://www.nature.com/genomics/human/watson-crick/ 'A structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid'] was published on [[April 25]]. Research uncovering the [[genetic code]] and how DNA relates to proteins followed not long after, in [[1957]]. These findings represent the birth of [[molecular biology]]. In [[1962]], Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the [[Nobel Prize]] for physiology/medicine, Franklin had died. In [[1988]], Watson was appointed as the Head of the [[Human Genome Project]] at the [[National Institutes of Health]]. See Also: [[RNA]] ==External addresses== *Google: [http://directory.google.com/Top/Science/Biology/Biochemistry_and_Molecular_Biology/Biomolecules/Nucleic_Acids/ Nucleic Acids] *[http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/sci/tech/2949629.stm 17 April, 2003, BBC News: Most ancient DNA ever?]
